feed.title: JNSKM
feed.subtitle: JNSKM is a blog, filled with cannot-forget information about how-to's, great products I bought or want to buy, and my thoughts and opinions about technology, culture, design, and other interesting topics.

entry.title: Learning Arduino.
entry.content:
I didn’t know Arduino is programmed using C. Thankfully a long long time ago when I was about 17 or 18 I took a summer class in C/C++. I was surprised I remembered a few things while looking at some C code.

Using an Arduino Uno, a Grove shield, a Grove dust sensor, and a Grove OLED display I was able to get the data off the dust sensor and display it on the OLED.

I was quite pleased. Looking forward to learning more things using the Arduino…
entry.summary: I didn’t know Arduino is programmed using C. Thankfully a long long time ago when I was about 17 or 18 I took a summer class in C/C++. I was surprised I remembered a few things while looking at some C code.

entry.title: Learning Python.
entry.content:
I got two books on Python. I want to learn Python to learn how to program, but in particular how to do data analysis, machine learning, and eventually artificial intelligence. I also want to learn how to control boards like Arduino and automate certain tasks using the Raspberry Pi.

There are so many things in this world that could be automated: figuring out when to water and how much depending on the season. Figuring out when to run the air conditioner. When to change the oil. Where to change the oil. When to schedule for an oil change. What clothing brands to buy? And when? What textile is good for you and for the environment. Where to buy groceries. When to buy certain groceries. How to cook. What to cook. When to eat. When to sleep. For how long. How to exercise. What to learn. When to learn. I mean there are so many things that somebody out there has figured out. And all I have to do is find the answers and automate the reminding process so that we focus on more important things.
entry.summary: I got two books on Python. I want to learn Python to learn how to program, but in particular how to do data analysis, machine learning, and eventually artificial intelligence. I also want to learn how to control boards like Arduino and automate certain tasks using the Raspberry Pi.

entry.title: Learning Statistics and Linear Algebra toward Machine Learning.

link.title: Learning Statistics and Linear Algebra toward Machine Learning.

entry.content:
I’ve been learning statistics and linear algebra. Mostly through Kahn Academy, YouTube, Coursera and a couple of other online sources. It’s been pretty great. When I’m learning something and I get stuck, I just open up another tab and search for that particular subject matter and learn that. Afterwards, I go back to my original learning and continue. There’s potential for jumping all over the place if I don’t have discipline and I’ve done that a little already, but with KA and Coursera it’s really easy to see your progress.

The ultimate goal for me is to learn how to program machine learning. I would like build robots that are able to identify the type of plant, figure out soil conditions and then recommend what needs to be done next to make sure the plant is getting all of its nutrients. Perhaps even connect to a water source to water it when necessary. When to harvest. When to seed. How to cook even. Other robots to determin water quality levels, air quality. Figure out how to do laundry. Dust levels on carpets and recommend vacuuming. Or make Roomba go to work. All sorts of things that make living more automated. When to make investments and what type of investments. What type of food to buy depending on the season and location.

What type clothing to buy that’s reliable, durable, a good value, and good for the environment and for the people who are involved in manufacturing the clothing. I’m thinking of robots that can make recommendations for almost everything that we need. Figure out when we have the most energy and what type of foods we need to eat based on our environment, family history, and DNA testing.

Home defence rebots. Sentinels. Communicators. Door defence augmentation. Active defence? Work with other people in the neighborhood to alert them and request help. Everything.

Continously learn from books, written by really smart people who know what they are talking about. Will limit knowledge gained from the internet unless it is a vetted source. For instance, financial information from sources like the Wall Street Journal and the Financial Times. Western standard medical information from places like WebMD. I think identifying reliable sources of information on the internet might be an important capability for a learning robot. What makes a source reliable? It might be simple: look at the author and determine if s/he is from a reputable institution and has relavant experience. Crosscheck. Read his comments and what she posts and says on social media.
entry.summary: I’ve been learning statistics and linear algebra. Mostly through Kahn Academy, YouTube, Coursera and a couple of other online sources. It’s been pretty great. When I’m learning something and I get stuck, I just open up another tab and search for that particular subject matter and learn that. Afterwards, I go back to my original learning and continue. There’s potential for jumping all over the place if I don’t have discipline and I’ve done that a little already, but with KA and Coursera it’s really easy to see your progress.

entry.title: Learning takes daily practice. And sleep.

link.title: Learning takes daily practice. And sleep.

entry.content:
Because of the way the brain works, I need to repeat something over and over, and over again for something new to stick. Or more accurately for something new I am trying to learn, for that to chunk and then to be stored in long term memory. But I can’t repeat right after one another. I need some time for my brain to process (chunk) what I am learning and that takes time. Sometimes it takes a nap but more often it takes an entire night of sleeping.

So there is really no way to rapidly learn something new. You need the daily cycle of learning and then sleeping for what you are learning to become part of your long-term memory. And when that happens what you are learning becomes something like second nature. You become extremely proficient at it.

Now chunking just means a grouping of things. Let’s take driving for example. Put the key in. Make sure the car is in park. Press the brake and then turn the key all the way clockwise until the engine starts. This I don’t think about on a step-by-step basis because it’s chunked in my long-term memory and it is more or less second nature, automatic. That is why I am really good at doing it. But then there are other, many other chunks, when it comes to driving a car. And to get all those chunks into long-term memory takes a long time and a lot of practice.

So that’s what I will do with Jekyll. I’m still learning the “front matter”. It will take a long time for me to get proficient and another long time for me to get really good at it and then another long time for me to get to a level where it is like breathing. Automatic.
entry.summary: Because of the way the brain works, I need to repeat something over and over, and over again for something new to stick. Or more accurately for something new I am trying to learn, for that to chunk and then to be stored in long term memory. But I can’t repeat right after one another. I need some time for my brain to process (chunk) what I am learning and that takes time. Sometimes it takes a nap but more often it takes an entire night of sleeping.

entry.title: Welcome to Jekyll!
entry.content:
You’ll find this post in your _posts directory. Go ahead and edit it and re-build the site to see your changes. You can rebuild the site in many different ways, but the most common way is to run jekyll serve, which launches a web server and auto-regenerates your site when a file is updated.

To add new posts, simply add a file in the _posts directory that follows the convention YYYY-MM-DD-name-of-post.ext and includes the necessary front matter. Take a look at the source for this post to get an idea about how it works.

Jekyll also offers powerful support for code snippets:

Check out the Jekyll docs for more info on how to get the most out of Jekyll. File all bugs/feature requests at Jekyll’s GitHub repo. If you have questions, you can ask them on Jekyll Talk.
entry.summary: You’ll find this post in your _posts directory. Go ahead and edit it and re-build the site to see your changes. You can rebuild the site in many different ways, but the most common way is to run jekyll serve, which launches a web server and auto-regenerates your site when a file is updated.

entry.title: I want to learn Jekyll.
entry.content:
I want to learn Jekyll. There are a few reasons why.

First, I wanted to see if I can learn something other than WordPress, which I have been using since 2008. WP is powerful, but it is also complicated. I still don’t quite understand PHP and mySQL. And recently I was not able to update to a new WP version because the PHP version used on my host was 5.5.x and it needed 5.6.x. I didn’t know how to update PHP version on my host, so I started looking elsewhere to blog. (I did eventually find out how to update the PHP version. I just needed to get to the particular subdirectory where WP was installed to upgrade the PHP version. I wasn’t that hard once I figured this little requirement out.)

Second, I liked the idea of my blog not having to need a database. A database-free blog? Sure, why not. Simpler is better I think.

Third, I wanted to get away from having to pay for hosting. This won’t happen since I have a couple of sites, but I think it will be good practice if I want to prepare for when I want to simplify my online presence in the form of websites and blogs.
entry.summary: I want to learn Jekyll. There are a few reasons why.
